Integration: from a temp `HOME`, run the pairing/install path that consumes these values against `wrangler dev` and assert the upserted machine record carries the stable fingerprint hash, the OS, and the auto label, and that a re-run upserts (does not duplicate) the same machine. Proof is the passing stability + no-leak + upsert-idempotency suite.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires the epic root `EPIC: agent-core (schema, queue, sender, tokens)` and `A-MONOREPO` (pnpm workspaces + build via tsup).
- Feeds pairing and machine upserts; the label is auto-detected here and user-editable in the Machines screen later.
- Invariants: fingerprint is hashed (non-reversible), no raw machine identifiers persisted or logged.

## References
- PRD §10.3 (session/machine identity inputs), §14.3 (`machine_installations`: `machine_fingerprint_hash`, `os`, `label`), §10.2 (`machine {label, os}` in payload), §8.7 (auto-detected, editable machine labels).
notes:
DONE + PROVEN. src/fingerprint.ts: getMachineFingerprintHash() (stable sha256 hex of durable signals — platform/arch/hostname/cpuModel/totalmem/primary-MAC — joined + hashed; ONLY the hash leaves, never raw identifiers; §14.3 machine_fingerprint_hash). collectMachineSignals() (injectable for tests), fingerprintFromSignals(). getOS(): darwin→macos, win32→windows, linux→linux, else platform (§10.2 machine.os). getMachineLabel(): hostname minus .local, non-empty fallback (§8.7 auto-detected/editable). getMachineIdentity() → {label, os, fingerprintHash} for the event machine block + machine-installation upsert.

PROOF (src/fingerprint.test.ts, 11 tests): stability (same signals → same hash, repeated calls equal; real-host collectMachineSignals stable); sha256 hex format; distinctness (different machines → different hashes); NO-LEAK (raw hostname + MAC absent from the hash); getOS mapping incl. running-CI-OS; label strips .local + non-empty fallback + real label non-empty; identity shape. Full gate + pre-push green.

DEFERRED: live wrangler-dev upsert-idempotency (cross-repo, needs pairing/machine-upsert endpoints) — unit tests prove stability/no-leak/OS/label that feed it.
close_reason: Machine fingerprint (stable non-reversible sha256 of durable signals; raw identifiers never leave), normalized getOS, auto getMachineLabel, getMachineIdentity. 11 tests: stability + no-leak + distinctness + OS/label. All gates green.

title: Codex — status() + doctor()
description:
Implement `status()` and `doctor()` for the Codex adapter so the CLI and app can report Codex integration health and the user can self-diagnose problems — including the Codex-specific trust state. This powers the `birdybeep status` / `birdybeep doctor` commands for Codex.

## Scope
- Implement `status(): Promise<IntegrationStatus>` for `id: "codex"`: report one of `installed | not_detected | needs_restart | needs_trust | error | revoked | unknown` (PRD §8.8, §14.4) based on detection, presence of BirdyBeep-managed entries, trust state, and whether a real event has been seen.
- Implement `doctor(): Promise<DoctorResult>`: actionable health checks — Codex detected? config readable + valid? BirdyBeep notify/hook entries present and pointing at `birdybeep hook codex`? trust granted (any event seen)? machine token resolvable? — each with a plain-language remedy (e.g. "Open Codex and run /hooks").
- `status()` returns `needs_trust` until the first real event arrives, consistent with `CX-TRUST`; returns `error` when config is present but malformed/broken.
- Both are read-only diagnostics: no config mutation.
- Non-goals: fixing/repairing config automatically; install/uninstall.

## Acceptance criteria
- `status()` returns the correct enum value for each fixture state: not installed → `not_detected`; installed-no-events → `needs_trust`; installed-with-event → `installed`; broken config → `error`.
- `doctor()` flags a missing/edited-away BirdyBeep hook entry and prints a specific remediation; flags an untrusted Codex with the `/hooks` instruction.
- Status strings match the `agent_integrations.status` enum exactly and are suitable for the integration status payload.
- Neither call writes to disk.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- CLI/adapter temp-HOME verification: construct fixture HOMEs for each state (absent Codex; installed-untrusted; installed-trusted via a delivered event against the stub API; corrupted config) and assert `status()` and `doctor()` return the correct enum/diagnoses for each. Inspect `doctor()` output strings for the expected remedies.
- Assert read-only (config unchanged after calls). Run as real vitest cases over actual temp dirs.
- Pre-push hook + CI hard-block merge unless all state cases pass.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `Codex — install notify command + hooks` (CX-INSTALL) to establish the installed/managed-entries baseline that status/doctor inspect; coordinates with `Codex — needs_trust handling` (CX-TRUST) for the trust state.
- Feeds the product `POST /v1/integrations/status` and Machine-detail UI (cross-repo NOTE). Status payloads carry metadata only — no notification content; paths hashed.

## References
- PRD §9.6 (Codex integration), §8.8 (integration status values), §14.4 (`agent_integrations`), §9.4 (`birdybeep status` / `doctor` commands), §21.1 (doctor as harness-change mitigation). Design: Machine detail / integrations (`app/11-machine-detail.png`).

title: Offline-queue drain + non-blocking E2E
description:
The mandatory reliability E2E for the offline path: with the backend unreachable a hook returns fast and queues the event; a later hook/status/doctor drains it and delivers — and the harness is never blocked or slowed. Proves the §9.3/§19.4 non-blocking promise with a real install.

## Scope
- Real-install scenario in a temp HOME: point the sender at an unreachable backend, fire a real hook, and assert the event is queued and the command returns fast without erroring the harness.
- Then restore reachability and run a later `hook`/`status`/`doctor`, asserting the queued event drains and is delivered to the API.
- Assert the harness is never blocked or noticeably slowed: measure that the offline hook returns within the fast budget.
- Use the E2E test harness helpers; exercise real queue, sender, and normalize code paths.
- Non-goals: guaranteed durable delivery beyond the 24h best-effort window; mobile/web reliability tests.

## Acceptance criteria
- With the backend unreachable, firing a hook writes the event to the local queue and returns fast (within the defined budget) without failing the harness.
- After connectivity is restored, a subsequent `hook`/`status`/`doctor` drains the queue and the event is delivered to `POST /v1/agent-events`.
- A timing assertion proves the offline hook did not block/slow the harness.
- The test runs green in CI and is enforced by the pre-push hook.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- This ticket IS the offline-reliability E2E. Concrete proof: in a temp HOME, install adapters, set the sender's target to an unreachable host, fire a real hook payload, and assert (1) the event is in the temp-HOME queue, (2) the command exits fast within the budget, (3) measured latency confirms non-blocking. Then make the stub/wrangler-dev reachable, run `birdybeep status` (and a later hook), and assert the queue drains and the event reaches the API. CI + pre-push enforce it.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `birdybeep hook claude|codex|opencode`, `birdybeep status`, the `Local event queue (24h, strict perms)`, and the `E2E test harness (temp HOME + stub API)`.
- Reliability invariants are the whole point: best-effort 24h queue, opportunistic drain, fast non-blocking return; the harness must never be blocked.

## References
- PRD §9.3 (no daemon, local queue, opportunistic drain, fast return), §19.4 (local event queue does not block harness execution). CLI is terminal-only — no Perch screen.

title: birdybeep agent uninstall all|<harness>
description:
`birdybeep agent uninstall all|<harness>` — cleanly removes BirdyBeep integration config, touching ONLY BirdyBeep-managed entries and restoring from backup, then verifying the harness config is left clean. Reversibility is a core trust promise for the open-source CLI.

## Scope
- Remove only BirdyBeep-managed entries from each targeted harness's user-level config; `all` covers every installed harness, `<harness>` targets one.
- Restore pre-install state from the backup created at install time where applicable, and verify the resulting config no longer references BirdyBeep and that no unrelated user entries were lost.
- Idempotent and safe when nothing is installed; report clearly per harness.
- Non-goals: clearing the local queue or logging out (separate commands), removing non-BirdyBeep config.

## Acceptance criteria
- `agent uninstall <harness>` removes only BirdyBeep-managed entries; all pre-existing user config remains byte-intact.
- After uninstall, the harness config contains no BirdyBeep references and the verification step passes.
- Running uninstall when not installed is safe and exits zero with a clear message.
- `all` uninstalls every installed harness; partial failures are reported per harness without aborting the others.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- CLI/adapter verification in a temp HOME against the E2E harness: in a temp HOME, run install (creating backups + managed entries on fixture configs that contain unrelated user content), then `agent uninstall all`, and assert the configs are restored to their pre-install bytes, contain zero BirdyBeep references, and that unrelated user entries are preserved. Run uninstall again and assert it's a safe no-op. Include a config-hand-edited-after-install case to prove only managed entries are stripped. Snapshot/diff the before/after configs.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `birdybeep agent install all|<harness>` (shares the adapter install/backup machinery and reverses it).
- Idempotency/backup invariants are load-bearing: uninstall removes only BirdyBeep-managed entries and restores from the install-time backup.

## References
- PRD §7.3 (acceptance: uninstall removes only BirdyBeep-managed config, backup before modification), §9.4, §16.4 (public repo must include uninstall docs + non-destructive config-patching tests). CLI is terminal-only — no Perch screen.

title: OpenCode — install (global plugin loading)
description:
Implement `install()` for OpenCode: configure user-level/global plugin loading so OpenCode picks up the BirdyBeep plugin, doing so non-destructively (preserve + back up existing config, add only BirdyBeep-managed entries), and print the restart instruction since OpenCode must relaunch to load the plugin (§7.3 example output).

## Scope
- Patch OpenCode's global/user config to reference the BirdyBeep plugin, writing only BirdyBeep-managed entries and leaving all existing user config intact.
- Back up the prior config before modifying (so OC-UNINSTALL can restore it) and print the list of changed files (§7.3).
- Print the post-install restart message: "Restart OpenCode for the plugin to load." and mark the integration `needs_restart` until a real event confirms loading.
- Idempotent: re-running install does not duplicate the plugin reference or re-corrupt config; a second run is a no-op diff.
- Non-goals: project-level install; writing any token into config.

## Acceptance criteria
- After install, OpenCode global config contains exactly one BirdyBeep-managed plugin reference and all pre-existing keys are byte-preserved aside from the managed addition.
- A backup of the original config exists before the write.
- Re-running install is idempotent (no duplicate entries, identical resulting config).
- The restart instruction is printed and the integration status is `needs_restart` pending first event.
- No durable token is written into OpenCode config or any repo-local file.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Adapter test in a temp HOME with a representative pre-existing OpenCode config: run install, assert the managed plugin reference is added, a backup file exists, unrelated keys are untouched, and changed-files output is printed. Run install twice and assert the second run produces an identical config (idempotency). Assert no token string is present anywhere under the temp HOME after install. Snapshot of the generated config is owned by OC-SNAPSHOT.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on OC-PLUGIN (the plugin package that this config references).
- Idempotency, backup, and add-only invariants are mandatory (§7.3). Tokens are never written into config or repo files; they stay in the CLI secure store.
- Verify the correct global plugin-loading config key/location against current OpenCode docs (https://opencode.ai/docs/plugins/) before patching.

## References
- PRD §9.7 (configure user-level/global plugin loading, restart instructions), §7.3 (install behavior, example output, idempotency/backup/add-only).

title: OpenCode — normalizeEvent mapping
description:
Implement the pure event-mapping function that turns raw OpenCode lifecycle events into canonical BirdyBeep events per the §9.7 table. This is the deterministic core the plugin consumes; keeping it pure and table-driven makes it easy to test exhaustively and patch when OpenCode changes its event shapes (§21.1).

## Scope
- Map per §9.7: `session.created` -> `session_started`, `session.updated` -> `session_active`, `session.status` -> the status-specific event, `session.idle` -> `agent_idle`, `session.error` -> `agent_failed`, `permission.asked` -> `approval_required`, `permission.replied` -> `permission_replied`, `tool.execute.before` -> `tool_started`, `tool.execute.after` -> `tool_finished`.
- Derive `status` (AgentSessionStatus from §10.4: e.g. `approval_required` -> `waiting_for_approval`, `agent_idle` -> `idle`, `agent_error` -> `failed`) and set `source_session_id`, `harness: "opencode"`, `harness_version`, and workspace fields into the canonical payload (§10.2).
- Run redaction/truncation via the shared normalizer so paths are hashed and title/body stay minimal; build only metadata for non-notifying events.
- Pure mapping only — no I/O, no network, no token access. The plugin calls this; this never calls the plugin.
- Non-goal: notification decision (that is the backend's job per §10.5), though the mapping must produce the event types those rules key off.

## Acceptance criteria
- Every OpenCode event in the §9.7 table maps to the exact BirdyBeep event type and resulting session status; unknown/unhandled events map to `custom` (or are dropped) without throwing.
- `session.status` resolves to the correct status-specific event based on the reported OpenCode status value.
- Absolute paths are hashed/redacted and no plaintext title/body is retained beyond what delivery needs; output validates against the agent-event Zod schema.
- Function is pure and deterministic: identical input yields identical output, no side effects.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Unit tests with one case per §9.7 row asserting `{event_type, status}`, plus: `session.status` variants resolving to distinct events, an unknown event handled safely, and a redaction case proving a cwd path is hashed (no plaintext path in output). Assert output passes the shared agent-event schema validation. Pure-logic tests, no network.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on the OpenCode epic root, the AgentAdapter interface (CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE) for `normalizeEvent` signature, and the Normalizer + redaction/truncation core (CORE-NORMALIZE) for path hashing and truncation. Also depends on OC-DETECT for the resolved harness version/context.
- Privacy invariant: hash paths, never carry full title/body further than needed; Private Mode is handled downstream but the mapping must not embed sensitive strings that would defeat it.
- Cross-repo note: the BirdyBeep event types and notification-default semantics are defined in PRD §10 and consumed by the private backend — keep the mapping aligned to that contract.

## References
- PRD §9.7 (OpenCode event mapping table), §10.1 (event types), §10.2 (canonical payload), §10.4 (session statuses), §10.5 (notification defaults).

title: Codex — normalizeEvent mapping
description:
Implement `normalizeEvent()` for Codex: pure mapping logic that turns raw Codex notify/hook payloads into canonical BirdyBeep events per the §9.6 table. This is the translation layer that makes Codex sessions look like every other harness to the backend.

## Scope
- Implement `normalizeEvent(input: unknown): Promise<BirdyBeepAgentEvent>` for `id: "codex"` (PRD §9.1).
- Map Codex surfaces to BirdyBeep event types per the §9.6 table:
  - `notify` command → `needs_input` or `agent_completed` (disambiguate by payload)
  - `SessionStart` → `session_started` / `session_resumed`
  - `PermissionRequest` → `approval_required`
  - `PostToolUse` → `tool_finished`
  - `SubagentStart` → `subagent_started`
  - `SubagentStop` → `subagent_completed`
  - `Stop` → `agent_completed`
- Set the resulting session status and notification-default per the mapping (e.g. `approval_required` → `waiting_for_approval`, notify yes; `tool_finished` → activity update, notify no) consistent with §10.1/§10.4/§10.5.
- Derive session identity from available Codex fields (source session id; best-effort from cwd / session context / time window when absent) and populate workspace fields, hashing paths (PRD §10.3, §15.6).
- Run payloads through the shared redaction/truncation normalizer before producing the canonical event; never emit raw notification title/body for server persistence.
- Pure mapping — independent of config install state; no filesystem or network side effects.
- Non-goals: installing config; sending events; trust handling.

## Acceptance criteria
- Each Codex surface in the §9.6 table maps to the exact BirdyBeep event type, session effect, and notification-default specified.
- A `notify` payload signaling completion maps to `agent_completed`; one signaling a question/blocked state maps to `needs_input`.
- Output validates against the canonical agent-event schema (§10.2) and uses only enum values from `BirdyBeepEventType` (§10.1) and `AgentSessionStatus` (§10.4).
- Absolute paths are hashed (`cwd_hash`) and no plaintext title/body is retained for persistence; session identity is stable across events of the same Codex session.
- Unknown/unsupported Codex inputs degrade safely (e.g. `custom`/`unknown`) without throwing.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- CLI/adapter unit verification with real fixture payloads: feed captured/representative Codex `notify`, `SessionStart`, `PermissionRequest`, `PostToolUse`, `SubagentStart`, `SubagentStop`, and `Stop` payloads through `normalizeEvent()` and assert exact event type, status, notify-default, and hashed-path output for each (table-driven vitest).
- Assert path-hashing and title/body non-persistence invariants in the produced event; assert stable session-id derivation across two events from one session.
- These mappings are exercised against the live wiring in `CX-E2E`; the pure unit cases gate this ticket. Pre-push hook + CI hard-block merge.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `AgentAdapter interface` (CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE) and `Normalizer + redaction/truncation` (CORE-NORMALIZE) for the canonical event types and the redaction pipeline; requires `Codex — detect()` (CX-DETECT) for harness/version context.
- Privacy invariants: hash paths, never persist/log notification title/body — only metadata + hashes flow server-side.

## References
- PRD §9.6 (Codex event mapping table), §10.1 (event types), §10.2 (canonical payload), §10.3 (session identity), §10.4 (session statuses), §10.5 (notification decision rules), §15.6 (Private Mode / redaction).

title: OpenCode — uninstall()
description:
Implement `uninstall()` for the OpenCode adapter: cleanly remove the BirdyBeep plugin reference from OpenCode's config and restore the backup taken at install time, touching nothing else. This is the reversibility guarantee the secondary persona (§6.2) explicitly requires and a hard acceptance criterion (§7.3, §19.2).

## Scope
- Remove only the BirdyBeep-managed plugin reference from OpenCode global config; leave every other user entry exactly as it was.
- Restore from the install-time backup where appropriate so the resulting config matches the pre-install state (modulo legitimate user changes since install).
- Be safe to run when nothing is installed (no-op, no throw) and idempotent on repeat invocation.
- Non-goals: removing the plugin package itself from disk if globally shared in unexpected ways beyond BirdyBeep's managed reference; revoking the machine token (that is a separate CLI/app flow).

## Acceptance criteria
- After uninstall, the BirdyBeep plugin reference is gone and all non-BirdyBeep config entries are byte-preserved.
- Running uninstall when not installed is a safe no-op.
- Running uninstall twice is idempotent and does not error.
- Post-uninstall, `status()` reports `not_detected` (or `installed:false`) for the BirdyBeep plugin reference.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Adapter test in a temp HOME: install (creating the managed reference + backup), then uninstall, and assert the managed reference is removed while unrelated keys remain untouched and match the pre-install config. Assert uninstall-when-absent is a no-op and a second uninstall does not throw. Verify the round-trip install -> uninstall leaves config equivalent to the original.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on OC-INSTALL (consumes the backup it created and the managed-entry markers it wrote).
- Add-only/remove-only invariant is mandatory: uninstall must remove only BirdyBeep-managed entries and restore the backup (§7.3). No token handling here; tokens live in the secure CLI store and are revoked separately.

## References
- PRD §7.3 (uninstall removes only BirdyBeep-managed entries, backup/restore), §6.2 (reversible installation), §19.2 (uninstall acceptance).

title: docs/troubleshooting.md
description:
`docs/troubleshooting.md` — the self-serve fix-it page for the common failure modes: integration not detected, Codex `needs_trust`, OpenCode `needs_restart`, offline/queued events, and token/network problems. It is built around real `birdybeep doctor` output so users can match symptoms to fixes.

## Scope
- `birdybeep doctor` walkthrough: what it diagnoses (token/integration/queue/network), the actionable fixes it prints, and how it drains the local queue (§9.4, `CLI-DOCTOR`).
- Per-harness states: Codex `needs_trust` (open Codex, run `/hooks`; not marked installed until first event — §9.6, §21.2) and OpenCode `needs_restart` (restart for the plugin to load — §9.7). Show how these surface in `doctor`/`status` output.
- Offline queue: events written to the local 24h queue when delivery fails; how the queue drains opportunistically on later hook/`status`/`doctor` invocations; `birdybeep queue clear` for a stuck queue (§9.3, §15.3).
- Common errors: expired/single-use pairing code, revoked machine token, push not arriving (link to in-app push status), harness not detected.
- Non-goals: install steps (lives in `docs/install.md`), security rationale (lives in `docs/security.md`).

## Acceptance criteria
- Page reproduces real `birdybeep doctor` output sections and maps each diagnostic to a fix.
- Page documents the `needs_trust` (Codex) and `needs_restart` (OpenCode) remediation steps accurately.
- Page documents the offline-queue behavior (24h retention, opportunistic drain, non-blocking) and `queue clear`.
- Every command shown runs and produces output matching the doc.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- In a temp HOME, induce each documented failure and confirm the doc's fix resolves it against real CLI behavior: (a) with no token, run `birdybeep doctor` and assert it reports the token problem and the documented fix; (b) install Codex config and assert `doctor`/`status` reports `needs_trust` until a first event, matching the doc; (c) install OpenCode and assert `needs_restart` is reported; (d) with the backend unreachable, fire a hook, assert it queues and returns fast (harness not blocked), then run `doctor` and assert the queue drains and delivers; (e) run `birdybeep queue clear` and assert the queue empties. Diff captured `doctor` output against the doc. Run the link-checker.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on **EPIC: Public docs & examples** (epic root) and **`birdybeep doctor`** (`CLI-DOCTOR`) — the page is structured around real `doctor` output and remediation, which must exist first.
- Invariants to document and verify: the local hook returns fast and never blocks/slows the harness; queue retention is 24h, best-effort, drained opportunistically; Codex stays `needs_trust` until first event; tokens never in repo files.

## References
- PRD §16.4 (troubleshooting docs requirement), §9.3 (no background daemon / offline queue), §9.4 (CLI commands incl. `doctor`, `status`, `queue clear`), §9.6–9.7 (Codex trust, OpenCode restart), §21.1–21.2 (harness API change + Codex trust mitigations), §15.3 (local retry queue).

title: docs/pairing.md
description:
`docs/pairing.md` — explains how `birdybeep login` pairs a machine to a user account via QR or manual code, and (critically for the trust story) why the QR is safe: it carries a short-lived pairing code, never a durable API token.

## Scope
- The pairing flow per §7.2: `birdybeep login` → CLI calls `pair/start` → displays QR + short pairing code + fallback verification URL → user scans in the app → app shows approval screen → CLI receives and securely stores the machine token.
- Manual / headless path: SSH and headless contexts pair by typing the short code at the fallback URL; document this prominently since many agent boxes are remote.
- Security of the QR: QR contains only short-lived pairing information (no durable token); pairing code is single-use; QR/pairing session expires after 10 minutes; machine token is shown/issued once and revocable from the mobile app (§7.2, §15.1). Cross-link to `docs/security.md`.
- Token storage note: OS keychain when possible, strict-permission file fallback, never in repo files — link to security docs for detail.
- Non-goals: agent-integration install (lives in `docs/install.md`), full token-handling deep dive (lives in `docs/security.md`).

## Acceptance criteria
- Page documents both QR and manual-code pairing, including the headless/SSH path via the fallback URL.
- Page states the 10-minute expiry, single-use pairing code, and that the QR carries no durable token.
- Documented `birdybeep login` output (QR rendering, short code, fallback URL) matches real CLI output.
- Page states the machine token is stored in the OS keychain (file fallback) and is revocable from the app, and is never written into repo files.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- In a temp HOME against `wrangler dev` (or staging), run `birdybeep login` in `--non-interactive`/manual mode and confirm the CLI renders a QR, a short code, and a fallback URL exactly as the doc shows; complete the pairing using the manual code path and assert a machine token is issued and stored in the configured store (keychain/file), with strict file permissions verified for the fallback. Assert the pairing code is rejected on a second use (single-use) and after the 10-minute window (expiry) per `PAIR-*` behavior. Grep the temp HOME and any repo dir to prove no token was written into a repo-local file. Run the link-checker over the page.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on **EPIC: Public docs & examples** (epic root) and **`birdybeep login` (QR + manual)** (`CLI-LOGIN`) — the page documents that command's real flow and output, which must exist first. CLI-LOGIN itself depends on product-side `PAIR-START`/`PAIR-APPROVE`/`PAIR-TOKEN-MINT`.
- Cross-repo note (verbatim from CLI-LOGIN): "product PAIR-START/PAIR-APPROVE/PAIR-TOKEN-MINT." The pairing endpoints are owned by the product repo; verify against `wrangler dev` of the product API.
- Invariants to document and verify: QR carries a short-lived code not a token; pairing code single-use; 10-minute expiry; token shown once, hash-only server-side, stored in keychain/strict-perm file, never in repo files.

## References
- PRD §16.4 (pairing docs requirement), §7.2 (machine pairing flow + acceptance criteria), §15.1 (token handling), §9.4 (CLI commands).

title: EPIC: Codex adapter (one-time trust)
description:
Ship the Codex adapter for the BirdyBeep public agent repo (`birdybeep-agent`, MIT): a complete `AgentAdapter` implementation that installs Codex's user-level `notify` command and lifecycle hooks, maps Codex surfaces to BirdyBeep events, and gracefully handles Codex's one-time hook-trust caveat. This is one of the three launch integrations and the only one with an expected manual trust step, so getting the `needs_trust` UX right is a primary risk-mitigation goal.

## Scope
- Deliver `detect()`, `install()`, `uninstall()`, `status()`, `doctor()`, and `normalizeEvent()` for `id: "codex"`, conforming to the shared `AgentAdapter` interface (PRD §9.1).
- Install user-level Codex `notify` command + lifecycle hooks; add only BirdyBeep-managed entries; preserve and back up existing config; idempotent re-runs (PRD §9.6, §7.3).
- Route every hook/notify invocation through the `birdybeep hook codex` local command pattern (token read securely, redact/truncate, send with short timeout, queue on failure, return fast) — no durable tokens in Codex config files (PRD §9.2).
- Map `notify` / `SessionStart` / `PermissionRequest` / `PostToolUse` / `SubagentStart` / `SubagentStop` / `Stop` to BirdyBeep events per the §9.6 table.
- Surface Codex's one-time hook trust: print copy-paste `/hooks` instructions, report `needs_trust` upstream, and do NOT mark the integration fully installed until the first real event arrives (PRD §21.2).
- Snapshot tests for generated config + non-destructive patching, plus a real E2E that installs into a temp HOME and asserts delivered events including the trust path.
- Non-goals: project-level install (user/global only), background daemon, Copilot/Gemini.

## Success criteria
- A user (or coding agent) runs `birdybeep agent install codex` on a machine with Codex present and the adapter installs the notify command + hooks non-destructively, prints the trust message, and reports `needs_trust` until a real Codex event lands — at which point status flips to `installed`.
- All 8 child tickets are merged: detect, install, trust handling, normalize mapping, status+doctor, uninstall, snapshot tests, and the mandatory real E2E.
- The Codex `needs_trust` state is observable end-to-end and is reflected by the product `agent_integrations` model and Machine-detail UI (cross-repo).
- Uninstall removes only BirdyBeep entries and restores the backup; re-install is idempotent; no token is ever written to a repo file.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Epic test bar: every child ticket is verified with real artifacts before push — no "it should work." Config behavior is locked by vitest snapshot tests over generated TOML/config and a non-destructive-patch test that asserts pre-existing user keys survive a round-trip.
- The capstone is `CX-E2E`: install the adapter into an isolated temp `HOME`, fire the real Codex `notify` payload and the real lifecycle-hook payloads against a stubbed BirdyBeep API (wrangler dev / stub from `A-TEST-HARNESS`), and assert the correct normalized events are delivered — explicitly covering the trust path (no event before trust → `needs_trust`; first event after trust → `installed`).
- CI + pre-push hook hard-block merge unless detect/install/uninstall/normalize unit tests, snapshot tests, and the real E2E all pass.

## Dependencies & notes
- Builds on `AgentAdapter interface` (CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE) and `Normalizer + redaction/truncation` (CORE-NORMALIZE) from agent-core, and the `E2E test harness (temp HOME + stub API)` (A-TEST-HARNESS).
- Cross-repo note: the product backend's `POST /v1/integrations/status` and Machine-detail UI must reflect `needs_trust` for Codex until the first event arrives. This is an xref (NOTES), not a hard link.
- Privacy/idempotency invariants apply throughout: installers are idempotent, back up existing config, add only BirdyBeep-managed entries; tokens are hash-only server-side and never written into config/repo files; paths are hashed; Private Mode is free for all users.

## References
- PRD §9.6 (Codex integration), §21.2 (Codex hook trust friction), §9.1–9.2 (adapter interface + local command pattern), §7.3 (agent installation flow), §16.3–16.4 (public agent repo), §19.2 (CLI/integration acceptance).
- Design: Machine detail / integrations screen (`app/11-machine-detail.png`) showing `needs_trust`; Session detail integration health hint (`app/09-session-detail.png`).

title: birdybeep test
description:
`birdybeep test` — sends a test event through the real sender path so a developer can confirm end-to-end delivery (and trigger a test Beep on their phone) right after pairing/install. Exercises the production code path, not a mock.

## Scope
- Construct a representative test event and push it through the real Sender (short timeout, queue-on-fail) so it traverses the same redaction/normalization and delivery path as a genuine hook.
- Surface the outcome clearly: delivered to backend vs queued (when offline), with the resulting decision where the API returns one.
- Drain the local queue opportunistically as part of the run (§9.3).
- Support `--json`.
- Non-goals: bypassing the sender to call the API directly; this must use the real path so it actually validates delivery.

## Acceptance criteria
- `birdybeep test` produces a delivered test event when online (the user receives a test Beep / a push job is enqueued server-side) and a clearly-reported queued result when offline.
- The event flows through the real Sender and normalizer/redaction, not a shortcut.
- Exit code reflects delivered vs queued vs hard failure; `--json` mirrors the result.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- CLI/adapter verification in a temp HOME against the E2E harness: with the stub/wrangler-dev reachable, run `birdybeep test` and assert the stub received a well-formed agent event on the real sender path and (where wired) that a push/test-beep job was enqueued. With the stub unreachable, assert the event is written to the local queue and the command still returns fast and reports "queued". Assert redaction/truncation was applied to the payload before send.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `CLI framework + global structure` and `Sender (short timeout, queue-on-fail)`.
- Cross-repo note (xref): exercises product `TESTBEEP-API` (`POST /v1/test-beep`) / `EVT-INGEST` (`POST /v1/agent-events`) — coordination NOTE, not a hard link.

## References
- PRD §9.4 (commands), §7.1 (onboarding test Beep). CLI is terminal-only — no Perch screen.

title: OpenCode — status() + doctor()
description:
Implement `status()` and `doctor()` for the OpenCode adapter so the app's integration screen and the `birdybeep doctor` command report accurate health — most importantly the `needs_restart` state that is OpenCode's defining setup quirk (the plugin loads only after a relaunch).

## Scope
- `status()` returns an `IntegrationStatus` mapping to the §8.8 values: `installed`, `not_detected`, `needs_restart`, `error`, `revoked`, `unknown` — driven by OC-DETECT, whether the managed plugin reference is present, and whether a real event has confirmed loading yet.
- `needs_restart` reporting: after install (or config change) but before the plugin has demonstrably loaded, report `needs_restart`; once a real OpenCode event is observed, report `installed`.
- `doctor()` runs concrete checks — OpenCode detected? plugin reference present and well-formed? config readable? restart pending? — and returns actionable findings (the restart hint, a re-install hint, a corrupted-config hint) for `birdybeep doctor` (§9.4).
- Non-goal: mutating config (doctor diagnoses, it does not silently rewrite).

## Acceptance criteria
- `status()` returns `not_detected` with no OpenCode, `needs_restart` after install before confirmed load, `installed` after a confirmed event, and `error` when the config is present but malformed.
- `doctor()` surfaces the restart instruction when a restart is pending and a clear remediation when the plugin reference is missing or the config is unreadable.
- Status values are exactly the §8.8 enum strings the app/integration screen expects.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Adapter tests in a temp HOME covering each branch: no OpenCode -> `not_detected`; installed-but-no-event -> `needs_restart`; after a delivered event -> `installed`; malformed managed config -> `error`. `doctor()` tests assert the exact actionable messages (restart pending, missing reference, unreadable config). Verify `birdybeep doctor` aggregates these findings. Pure read-only checks against stubbed install layouts.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on OC-INSTALL (status reflects the installed plugin reference and the restart-pending state it produces).
- The §8.8 status enum is the cross-repo contract consumed by the private backend's `agent_integrations.status` and the app integration screen — return exactly those strings.
- Read-only: doctor reports problems, it does not rewrite config.

## References
- PRD §9.7 (health + restart reporting), §8.8 (integration status values), §9.4 (`birdybeep doctor`), §21.1 (doctor as harness-change mitigation).

title: OpenCode — plugin package
description:
Build the BirdyBeep OpenCode plugin package: the code that loads inside OpenCode, subscribes to `session.*`, `permission.*`, and `tool.*` events, runs them through OC-NORMALIZE, and hands them to the BirdyBeep sender via the local hook command. This is the live integration that makes OpenCode sessions chirp.

## Scope
- A publishable OpenCode plugin (per OpenCode's plugin API) that registers handlers for `session.created/updated/status/idle/error`, `permission.asked/replied`, and `tool.execute.before/after`.
- For each event: call the pure OC-NORMALIZE mapping, then invoke the `birdybeep hook opencode` path so the shared Sender reads the machine token securely, redacts/truncates, sends with a short timeout, and queues on failure — keeping handlers fast and non-blocking (§9.2, §9.3).
- The plugin consumes OC-NORMALIZE (mapping logic lives there); the plugin owns event subscription, the hook-command invocation, and not slowing OpenCode.
- Non-goals: embedding any durable token in the plugin or OpenCode config (token stays in the CLI's secure store); project-level loading.

## Acceptance criteria
- Plugin loads in OpenCode and registers all §9.7 event handlers without errors.
- Each handled event produces exactly one normalized BirdyBeep event routed through the sender; handlers return quickly and never block OpenCode's main loop.
- No durable token is present in the plugin or any config it touches; the sender resolves the token from the secure CLI store at call time.
- Send failures are swallowed into the local queue (§9.3) and do not surface as OpenCode errors.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Adapter/CLI test in a temp HOME: load the plugin module and fire real OpenCode-shaped `session.created`, `permission.asked`, `session.idle`, and `tool.execute.after` payloads at it; assert each yields the correct normalized event delivered to the stub API (the delivered-event assertion), and that a forced send failure routes the event to the local queue instead of throwing. Confirm no token string appears in any file the plugin writes/reads. (Full in-process OpenCode load is exercised by OC-E2E.)

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on OC-DETECT (resolved plugin path/version), the Sender with short timeout + queue-on-fail (CORE-SENDER), and OC-NORMALIZE (the mapping it consumes).
- Privacy/security invariants: tokens never written to config or repo files; paths hashed; title/body never logged. Hook command must not noticeably slow OpenCode (§9.3).
- Verify plugin registration API against current OpenCode plugin docs (https://opencode.ai/docs/plugins/) before implementing.

## References
- PRD §9.7 (plugin preference + event mapping), §9.2 (local command hook pattern), §9.3 (no daemon, queue-on-fail, return fast), §9.4 (`birdybeep hook opencode`).

title: README (value, install, uninstall, security)
description:
The top-level public README for `birdybeep-agent` — the first thing a developer or auditor reads before installing BirdyBeep into their coding environment. It must answer "what is this, why would I run it, what does it touch, and how do I get rid of it" in one scroll, and establish the MIT/open-source trust story.

## Scope
- Value prop: BirdyBeep is the mobile notification layer for AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode); "install once per machine, walk away, get a Beep when an agent needs you" (§1, §4).
- Quickstart: `npm install -g @birdybeep/cli`, `birdybeep login` (QR), `birdybeep agent install all`, `birdybeep test` — the exact commands from §7.2–7.3 / §9.4.
- Uninstall section: `birdybeep agent uninstall all` removes only BirdyBeep-managed entries and restores backed-up config (§7.3).
- "What data is sent" summary with a link to `docs/security.md`; "how tokens are stored" summary (OS keychain, strict-perm file fallback, never in repo files) linking to security/pairing docs.
- MIT license badge + statement; links to the five `docs/*.md` pages and `examples/`.
- Non-goals: full per-harness install detail (lives in `docs/install.md`), exhaustive security detail (lives in `docs/security.md`) — README links out rather than duplicating.

## Acceptance criteria
- README states value prop, install, uninstall, what-is-sent summary, token-storage summary, and MIT license, each in its own clearly headed section.
- Every command shown is the real, current CLI invocation and runs without error against the shipped `CLI-SHELL`.
- Token-storage and what-is-sent summaries are consistent with `docs/security.md` (no contradictions); all internal links resolve.
- README explicitly says machine tokens are shown once and never written into repo-local files, and that the QR code carries only a short-lived pairing code.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- In a temp HOME with the built CLI on PATH, run each command block in the README (`birdybeep --version`, `birdybeep --help`, `birdybeep login --help`, `birdybeep agent install --help`, `birdybeep test --help`) and assert exit code 0 and that the help/usage text matches what the README claims. Run a markdown link-checker over README.md and assert zero broken internal links. Diff the README's quickstart command list against `birdybeep --help` output to catch drift. Confirm the privacy claims by grepping the CLI/token-store behavior (token never written under any repo path) — assert the README's statement is literally true.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on **EPIC: Public docs & examples** (epic root) and **CLI framework + global structure** (`CLI-SHELL`) — the README documents the command surface that CLI-SHELL defines (config dir, `--json`/`--non-interactive`, `version`, `help`), so it must reflect the real framework.
- Privacy invariants to state correctly: token shown once, hash-only server-side, never in repo files; QR carries a short-lived code, not a token; installers are idempotent, back up config, and add only BirdyBeep-managed entries.

## References
- PRD §16.4 (public agent repo requirements), §1/§4 (value prop, product promise), §7.2–7.3 (install/pairing commands), §9.4 (CLI commands), §15.1 (token handling).

title: Codex — detect()
description:
Implement `detect()` for the Codex adapter so the installer knows whether Codex is present, which version it is, and where its user-level config lives. This is the gate that decides whether `birdybeep agent install codex` proceeds and is the first call the orchestrating CLI makes.

## Scope
- Implement `detect(): Promise<DetectionResult>` for `id: "codex"` per the shared `AgentAdapter` interface (PRD §9.1).
- Detect a Codex install on the current machine (resolve the Codex binary/CLI on PATH and/or known install locations), capture its version string, and resolve the user-level Codex config path (e.g. the `~/.codex` config home / config-reference location).
- Return a structured result: detected (bool), `harness_version`, resolved config path, and a not-detected reason when absent — enough for `install()`, `status()`, and the product `agent_integrations.harness_version` field.
- Pure, read-only detection: must not write, create, or mutate any Codex config (that is `CX-INSTALL`'s job).
- Respect `$HOME` / `$CODEX_HOME`-style overrides so detection works inside a temp HOME (required by the E2E harness).
- Non-goals: installing anything; trust handling; event mapping.

## Acceptance criteria
- On a machine with Codex installed, `detect()` returns detected=true with a non-empty version and a valid, existing config-directory path.
- On a machine without Codex, `detect()` returns detected=false with a clear, non-throwing reason (maps to integration status `not_detected`).
- Detection honors a custom HOME/config-home env override and resolves paths under it (no hard-coded absolute user paths).
- No filesystem writes occur during `detect()` (verified — directory mtimes/contents unchanged).

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- CLI/adapter verification in a temp HOME: (1) seed a fake Codex install + config dir under a temp `HOME`/`CODEX_HOME` and assert `detect()` returns detected=true, correct version, and the resolved path; (2) point at an empty temp HOME and assert detected=false with the `not_detected` reason; (3) assert the temp config dir is byte-identical before/after the call (read-only proof).
- Run as real vitest cases, not mocks of the filesystem where avoidable — use an actual temp dir created by the test.
- Pre-push hook + CI must pass these before merge.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `AgentAdapter interface` (CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE) for `DetectionResult` typing and the adapter contract.
- Under the `EPIC: Codex adapter (one-time trust)` epic root.
- The resolved config path/version feeds the product `agent_integrations` record; keep paths internal and avoid logging absolute paths verbatim (hash where surfaced) per privacy invariants.

## References
- PRD §9.6 (Codex integration), §9.1 (AgentAdapter interface), §8.8 / §14.4 (`agent_integrations`, including `not_detected` status and `harness_version`). Codex config-reference is the source for the config path.
notes:
DONE + PROVEN. Built the Codex adapter skeleton (mirrors the Claude Code adapter): src/paths.ts (codexConfigDir honoring $CODEX_HOME else ~/.codex; codexConfigFile=config.toml; HOME-relative), src/detect.ts (detectCodex: side-effect-free, never throws; detected if config dir exists OR 'codex --version' resolves; injectable home/codexHome/probeVersion), src/adapter.ts (codexAdapter: AgentAdapter — detect live; install/uninstall/status/doctor/normalizeEvent rejecting stubs naming their CX tickets). Replaced the A-MONOREPO placeholder.

PROOF (src/detect.test.ts, 5 tests, hermetic temp HOME, CODEX_HOME managed for hygiene): detect via seeded ~/.codex + probed version + configPath under sandbox; detect via binary alone; not-detected (no throw, no files); honors a $CODEX_HOME override; READ-ONLY (config dir byte-identical before/after via assertTreesEqual). Full gate + pre-push green.
close_reason: Codex detect(): HOME/$CODEX_HOME-relative, side-effect-free, never-throws; detected via ~/.codex or codex --version; read-only proven. 5 hermetic tests. Gate green.

title: Codex — config snapshot tests
description:
Add snapshot tests that lock the exact Codex config BirdyBeep generates and prove the install patch is non-destructive. These are an explicit PRD requirement for the public agent repo and a primary defense against silent breakage when Codex's config format shifts.

## Scope
- Snapshot the generated Codex config produced by `install()` (the BirdyBeep `notify` command + hook entries) so any unintended change to the emitted config fails CI loudly (PRD §16.4, §21.1).
- Non-destructive-patch tests: start from representative pre-existing Codex configs (with unrelated user keys, comments where the format allows, and differing key orders) and assert that after `install()` only BirdyBeep entries are added and all user content is preserved; after `uninstall()` the config returns to the original (round-trip snapshot).
- Cover idempotency: snapshot that a second `install()` yields no diff.
- Snapshots must be deterministic — normalize/strip volatile fields (timestamps, absolute temp-HOME paths, backup filenames) so snapshots are stable across machines.
- Non-goals: live event delivery (that is `CX-E2E`); UI snapshots.

## Acceptance criteria
- A committed snapshot captures the canonical generated BirdyBeep Codex config; an intentional change to the generated config requires an explicit snapshot update (reviewable diff).
- Non-destructive-patch test passes: user keys preserved on install; original restored on uninstall (round-trip).
- Idempotent-install snapshot shows zero diff on second install.
- Snapshots contain no machine-specific noise (no absolute user paths, no timestamps) and no tokens/secrets.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Pure-test ticket: implemented as `@cloudflare/vitest`/vitest snapshot cases (`toMatchSnapshot`) over configs generated in an actual temp `HOME`. Proof it works: run the suite, confirm snapshots are created/matched; deliberately perturb the generator in a scratch run to confirm the snapshot fails (then revert).
- Assert no secret/token string and no absolute temp path leaks into any committed snapshot.
- Pre-push hook + CI hard-block merge unless the snapshot suite passes.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires `Codex — install notify command + hooks` (CX-INSTALL) (and exercises the `CX-UNINSTALL` round-trip) to have generated config to snapshot.
- Directly satisfies the PRD §16.4 public-repo requirement for "examples of generated config" and "tests for non-destructive config patching," and the §21.1 mitigation of adding snapshot tests for config generation.
- Privacy invariant: snapshots are metadata/config only, never tokens or notification content; paths normalized.

## References
- PRD §16.4 (public agent repo requirements: examples of generated config + non-destructive patching tests), §21.1 (harness-change mitigation via snapshot tests), §9.6 (Codex install behavior).

title: Claude Code — status() + doctor()
description:
Implements `status()` and `doctor()` for the Claude Code adapter so the app's Integrations screen and `birdybeep doctor` can show whether the integration is healthy and tell the user exactly how to fix it when it isn't.

## Scope
- Implement `status(): Promise<IntegrationStatus>` returning one of the §8.8 values relevant to Claude Code: `installed`, `not_detected`, `needs_restart`, `error`, or `unknown`. Derive it from CC-DETECT (present + version) and the state of `~/.claude/settings.json` (are the BirdyBeep hook entries present and well-formed?).
- Implement `doctor(): Promise<DoctorResult>` that diagnoses common Claude Code integration problems and returns actionable fixes: Claude Code not installed, settings.json missing/unparseable, BirdyBeep hooks absent or partially installed (suggest re-run install), hooks present but malformed, settings file not writable.
- Surface `harness_version` and `adapter_version` in the status payload so the backend's integration record (and the Machines/Integrations screen) reflects real versions.
- Non-goals: no remote reporting here (the CLI's report-status command posts these results); no auto-repair beyond clear guidance.

## Acceptance criteria
- `status()` returns `installed` when Claude Code is present and BirdyBeep hooks are correctly registered; `not_detected` when Claude Code is absent; `needs_restart`/`error`/`unknown` in the corresponding degraded states.
- `doctor()` correctly identifies each seeded failure mode and returns a human-actionable fix string for it.
- Status payload includes `harness_version` and `adapter_version`.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
Unit tests over a temp HOME across states: (1) fully installed → `status()` = `installed`, doctor reports healthy; (2) Claude Code absent → `not_detected`; (3) settings.json present but BirdyBeep hooks removed → doctor flags missing hooks with a re-install fix; (4) corrupt/unparseable settings.json → `error` with a clear remediation; (5) read-only settings file → doctor flags permissions. Assert exact status enum + presence of the actionable fix. CI/pre-push hard-block on green.

## Dependencies & notes
- Requires "Claude Code — install user hooks" (status/doctor inspect the installed hook state and reuse the detect/path logic it depends on).
- The `IntegrationStatus` values must match the §8.8 enum the app renders; Claude Code does not use `needs_trust` (that is the Codex path).

## References
- PRD §9.5 (Claude Code integration), §8.8 (agent integration status values).
notes:
DONE + PROVEN. src/status.ts: claudeCodeStatus()/claudeCodeDoctor() wired into the adapter (all 6 AgentAdapter methods now live), + claudeCodeStatusReport() for the CLI/backend. status() (§8.8): not_detected (CC absent) / installed (all 6 BB hooks present+well-formed) / error (settings unparseable OR partial install) / unknown (CC present, BB not installed). Detection injectable (StatusOptions.detect) so tests don't shell out to claude --version. doctor() → DoctorResult with actionable remedies for: CC not installed, settings.json invalid JSON, BB hooks absent/partial (re-install fix), settings not writable. statusReport carries harnessVersion (from detect) + adapterVersion (CLAUDE_CODE_ADAPTER_VERSION). CC never uses needs_trust/needs_restart (Codex/OpenCode paths).

PROOF (src/status.test.ts, 11 tests, hermetic temp HOME, injected detect): status installed/not_detected/unknown/error(corrupt)/error(partial — removed one event's BB entry); statusReport has harness+adapter versions; doctor healthy when installed, and flags each seeded failure (CC absent / missing hooks / corrupt JSON / read-only file POSIX) with a remedy string. Full gate + pre-push green.
close_reason: Claude Code status()+doctor(): §8.8 status from detect+settings state (installed/not_detected/error/unknown), doctor diagnoses each failure mode with actionable fixes, statusReport carries harness+adapter versions. 11 hermetic tests. Gate green.

title: examples/{claude-code,codex,opencode}
description:
`examples/{claude-code,codex,opencode}` — committed, real samples of the config each adapter's installer produces, so an auditor can see exactly what BirdyBeep writes into their harness config before running the installer. This is the concrete "examples of generated config" requirement from §16.4 and a key trust artifact.

## Scope
- One directory per launch harness containing the BirdyBeep-managed config entries that `birdybeep agent install <harness>` writes: Claude Code user `settings.json` hook entries calling `birdybeep hook claude` (§9.5, `CC-INSTALL`); Codex notify command + lifecycle hook entries with the trust note (§9.6, `CX-INSTALL`); OpenCode global plugin-loading config + restart note (§9.7, `OC-INSTALL`).
- Each example shows ONLY the BirdyBeep-managed additions (clearly delimited), so it's obvious the installer is non-destructive and adds nothing else; include a short README per dir pointing back to `docs/install.md` and `docs/security.md`.
- Examples are kept in lockstep with the real generated output — they are the same artifacts the adapters' snapshot tests assert against, not stale hand-written approximations.
- Non-goals: full harness config files (only the BirdyBeep-managed portion), backend payload examples (that's `docs/security.md`).

## Acceptance criteria
- `examples/claude-code`, `examples/codex`, and `examples/opencode` each exist with the generated BirdyBeep-managed config for that harness.
- Each example matches what the corresponding installer actually writes (no drift from real output).
- Each example clearly marks the BirdyBeep-managed entries and includes the per-harness caveat (Codex trust, OpenCode restart) where relevant.
- Each dir links back to install/security docs.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- For each harness, run `birdybeep agent install <harness>` into a temp HOME with a fixture base config, extract the BirdyBeep-managed entries the installer added, and diff (normalized) against the committed `examples/<harness>` file — any difference fails. Tie this to the adapters' snapshot tests (`CC-SNAPSHOT`/`CX-SNAPSHOT`/`OC-SNAPSHOT`) so the examples and snapshots share a source of truth and drift is caught in CI. Confirm uninstall removes exactly the entries shown in the example (proving the example is the complete managed footprint). Run the link-checker over each dir's README.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on **EPIC: Public docs & examples** (epic root) and the three installers — **Claude Code — install user hooks** (`CC-INSTALL`), **Codex — install notify command + hooks** (`CX-INSTALL`), and **OpenCode — install (global plugin loading)** (`OC-INSTALL`) — since the examples are literally their output.
- Invariants to demonstrate in the examples: only BirdyBeep-managed entries are added (existing config preserved/backed up), installs are idempotent, and no tokens appear in any example config (tokens live in the keychain/strict-perm file, never in repo/harness config files).

## References
- PRD §16.3 (`examples/{claude-code,codex,opencode}` layout), §16.4 (examples of generated config requirement), §9.5–9.7 (per-harness install behavior + config), §7.3 (install flow, non-destructive + backup), §15.1 (no durable tokens in config files).

title: [HUMAN] Public repo settings + CI secrets + protection
description:
> ⚠️ **HUMAN REQUIRED** — a human must perform this (real account / secret / billing / store / production action). Agents must NOT attempt it; prepare everything up to this gate and stop.

Hardens the public `birdybeep-agent` GitHub repository so the open-source CLI and adapters are safe to accept contributions and so the E2E CI matrix can run against staging: branch protection, CODEOWNERS, issue/PR templates, and the CI secrets (staging API URL + token). An agent authors all the repo-config-as-files and documents the exact settings; a human applies the GitHub settings that require repo-admin rights and pastes the real secrets.

## What the human does vs. what an agent prepares
- **Human only:** enable branch protection on the default branch (require PR review, require the CI matrix status checks to pass, no force-push/deletes), confirm CODEOWNERS review enforcement, and add repository/Actions **secrets** — the staging API base URL and a scoped staging machine/installation token used by the E2E harness. These are admin/secret actions an agent must not perform.
- **Agent prepares (and stops at the gate):** commit `.github/CODEOWNERS`, issue templates and `PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md`, a `CONTRIBUTING.md`/`SECURITY.md`, the CI workflow that references the secret names (e.g. `STAGING_API_URL`, `STAGING_AGENT_TOKEN`) **by name only**, and a runbook listing the exact protection rules and secret names/values-format the human must set. The agent never holds or commits the real secret values.

## Scope
- Branch protection on the default branch: required PR review, required passing status checks wired to the `CI matrix (macOS/Ubuntu/Windows)` jobs, linear/no-force-push as appropriate.
- `CODEOWNERS` so adapter/CLI/security-sensitive paths require owner review.
- Issue templates (bug / integration request) and a PR template appropriate to an open-source adapter repo.
- CI secrets for E2E: a staging API URL and a short-lived/scoped staging token so the matrix can run real harness payloads against `wrangler dev`-style staging without exposing production.
- Non-goal: product-repo (`birdybeep`) settings, store credentials, or production backend secrets — this is the public MIT agent repo only.

## Acceptance criteria
- The default branch is protected: PRs cannot merge without review and without the required CI matrix checks passing; direct/force pushes to it are blocked.
- `CODEOWNERS`, issue templates, and PR template are present and active (GitHub shows owners auto-requested and templates offered on new issues/PRs).
- CI secrets `STAGING_API_URL` and the staging token exist as repo/Actions secrets and the E2E matrix job can read them (verified by a green E2E run, not by reading the values).
- No secret value is present in the repo, workflow files, or git history — workflows reference secrets by name only.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Before the gate (agent): validate the workflow + template files locally — lint the YAML, run `actionlint`/equivalent, and confirm the workflow references only `${{ secrets.STAGING_API_URL }}` / `${{ secrets.STAGING_AGENT_TOKEN }}` by name (grep proves no inline values).
- Before the gate (agent): in a temp HOME, perform a real CLI install and fire real harness payloads (Claude Code / Codex / OpenCode adapter events) against a **local** staging endpoint to prove the E2E harness asserts a delivered/ingested event — so the only missing piece for CI is the human-supplied secret.
- After the gate (agent, post-config verification): trigger the E2E CI matrix on a PR and confirm it goes green using the human-added secrets (proving the staging URL/token are wired); via the GitHub API, read back that branch protection rules, required checks, CODEOWNERS enforcement, and the named secrets exist — without reading secret values.
- "It should be protected" is not acceptable: the evidence is a merge blocked by a failing required check + an API readback of the protection/secret configuration + a green E2E run that consumed the secrets.

## Dependencies & notes
- Prerequisite — `CI matrix (macOS/Ubuntu/Windows)`: the cross-OS GitHub Actions workflow (install, lint, typecheck, unit, snapshot) must exist so its jobs can be set as required status checks and so the E2E job has somewhere to consume the staging secrets.
- Gate — child of `EPIC: Manual — HUMAN REQUIRED (publish & repo)`.
- Invariants: the staging token is hash-only / scoped and short-lived where possible, lives only in GitHub secrets (never in repo files), and the E2E harness must not log notification title/body — paths/IDs are hashed per the privacy rules. This is the public MIT repo whose code runs in users' dev environments, so CODEOWNERS gating on adapter/security paths is load-bearing for trust.

## References
- PRD §16.3 (public repo `birdybeep-agent`, MIT).
- PRD §16.4 (public agent repo requirements — clear install/uninstall/security docs, `doctor`, tests for non-destructive config patching; supports the templates/CODEOWNERS/contribution surface).
- PRD §21.1 (keep the public integration repo easy to patch/release with snapshot tests — the CI matrix these protections enforce).

title: scripts/smoke-test.ts
description:
The `scripts/smoke-test.ts` post-publish check that installs the published `@birdybeep/cli` in a clean, isolated environment and runs `birdybeep doctor` — the end-to-end proof that what was shipped actually works for a real consumer, not just on the build machine. This is the agentic-first guard against "it published, so it should work."

## Scope
- `scripts/smoke-test.ts` provisions an isolated environment (clean temp HOME and/or fresh temp project with empty `node_modules`, no repo `node_modules` on resolution path), installs the CLI as a real consumer would (`npm install -g @birdybeep/cli` against the target registry, or install the packed tarball), and exercises it.
- Runs `birdybeep doctor` (the §16.4-required diagnostic) and a minimal sanity surface (`birdybeep --version`, `birdybeep status` in an unpaired state) asserting expected exit codes and output — `doctor` must run and report cleanly in a fresh, unauthenticated environment (no machine token present).
- Supports two targets: a local/throwaway registry (Verdaccio) for CI rehearsal, and the real npm package post-publish for release verification.
- Asserts no secret/credential is required or written: in a fresh HOME with no keychain entry / token file, the CLI still starts and `doctor` runs; tokens are never sourced from repo files.
- Cleans up the temp HOME / global install afterward; non-zero exit on any failed step so a release agent treats a failed smoke as a failed release.
- Non-goal: full harness adapter event delivery (that is the adapter E2E tickets firing real harness payloads against `wrangler dev`); this ticket verifies the published artifact installs and `doctor` runs.

## Acceptance criteria
- In a clean temp HOME with the published (or packed) CLI installed, `birdybeep doctor` runs and exits 0 (or its defined healthy code) with no machine token and no repo on the path.
- `birdybeep --version` reports the just-published version; `birdybeep status` handles the unpaired/unauthenticated state gracefully.
- The script fails loudly (non-zero) if install fails, the binary is missing from `PATH`, or `doctor` errors — and it leaves no residue (temp HOME / global install removed).
- Runs against both a local registry (CI) and the real package (post-publish).

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
This ticket *is* the test harness, so its own proof is running it green against a freshly built artifact. Concrete proof: in CI, `pnpm pack`/publish `@birdybeep/cli` to a local Verdaccio registry, then run `scripts/smoke-test.ts` pointed at that registry — it must create a clean temp HOME with no token, `npm install -g` the CLI, run `birdybeep doctor` to exit 0, run `--version`/`status`, and tear the environment down. Add a negative check: a deliberately broken package (e.g. missing `bin`) makes the smoke script exit non-zero. The real-registry mode is invoked by REL-SCRIPT after an actual publish so a broken public release is caught immediately.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on "Build/bundle + package exports + bin" (REL-BUILD) for an installable, `bin`-exposing artifact; invoked by the publish script "scripts/release.ts" (REL-SCRIPT) after a real publish.
- Privacy/security invariant: smoke runs in a fresh HOME with no token — verifying the CLI never depends on secrets baked into the package or repo files, consistent with the public MIT trust model.
- Relies on `birdybeep doctor` existing as the diagnostic command (§9.4, §16.4).

## References
- PRD §16.3 (`scripts/smoke-test.ts` in the public agent repo).
- PRD §16.4 (public-repo requirement for a `doctor` command and that tokens are not written into repo files).
- PRD §9.4 (`birdybeep doctor`, `birdybeep status` command surface).

title: EPIC: Release tooling
description:
Root epic for the public `birdybeep-agent` repo's release pipeline: deterministic builds, version management, the publish script, and the post-publish smoke test that proves a real npm consumer can install and run the CLI. This is what turns merged adapter/CLI code into a published `@birdybeep/cli` (and its sibling packages) that AI-native developers can `npm install -g` and trust, since the repo is public and MIT-licensed.

## Success criteria
- A maintainer (or an autonomous agent) can take a green `main`, run the release flow, and publish correct, versioned npm packages with generated changelogs — no manual file surgery.
- Every published package has correct `exports`, `bin`, `engines`, and a `files` allowlist so it works in a clean consumer environment with no dev-only or secret files leaked.
- Publishing is dry-run by default; a real publish is an explicit, deliberate action.
- The pipeline self-verifies end to end: after a (dry-run or real) publish, the smoke test installs the actual published tarball/package in an isolated environment and runs `birdybeep doctor` successfully. "It should publish fine" is never accepted — the smoke test is the proof.
- Nothing secret is ever bundled or published; tokens are never written into repo files (the CLI sources them at runtime), consistent with the repo's open-source trust promise.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
Epic-level bar: the release pipeline is only considered working when, in CI and locally, (1) `tsup` builds all packages clean, (2) a dry-run release produces the expected version bumps + changelog without touching the registry, and (3) `scripts/smoke-test.ts` installs the packed CLI into a clean temp HOME / fresh `node_modules` and runs `birdybeep doctor` to a passing exit code with no network credentials. Each child ticket carries its own concrete verification; this epic closes only when all four children are green and the smoke test passes against a freshly packed artifact.

## Dependencies & notes
- Child tickets: Build/bundle + package exports + bin (REL-BUILD), Versioning via changesets (REL-VERSION), the publish script `scripts/release.ts` (REL-SCRIPT), and the post-publish smoke test `scripts/smoke-test.ts` (REL-SMOKE).
- Privacy/security invariant for the whole epic: the public agent repo must never bundle, log, or publish durable tokens or secrets; the CLI reads the machine token from the OS keychain / strict-perm file at runtime, never from a committed file.
- This is the public MIT repo (`birdybeep-agent`), so build artifacts and package metadata are part of the auditable surface developers inspect before installing.

## References
- PRD §16.3 (public repo `birdybeep-agent` layout: `packages/cli`, `agent-core`, `claude-code`, `codex`, `opencode`; `scripts/release.ts`, `scripts/smoke-test.ts`).
- PRD §16.4 (public repo requirements: install/uninstall docs, `doctor` command, tokens never in repo files).
- PRD §5.1 item 14 (CLI/integrations open source under MIT) and §9.4 (CLI command surface).

title: docs/adapter-development.md
description:
`docs/adapter-development.md` — the contributor guide for building a new harness adapter against the `AgentAdapter` interface. It lets community contributors (and future BirdyBeep) add support for a new coding agent without reverse-engineering the codebase, which is core to the open-source, auditable, community-contribution rationale (§16.1).

## Scope
- The `AgentAdapter` contract: document each method — `detect()`, `install()`, `uninstall()`, `status()`, `doctor()`, `normalizeEvent()` — with the expected input/output types and the `id`/`displayName` fields (§9.1, `CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE`).
- The event mapping responsibility: how `normalizeEvent()` maps a harness's native events to `BirdyBeepEventType` (§10.1) and produces the canonical payload (§10.2); reference the existing Claude/Codex/OpenCode mapping tables (§9.5–9.7) as worked examples.
- Non-destructive config patterns: installers must add only BirdyBeep-managed entries, back up existing config, be idempotent, and write tokens only via the shared token store (never into repo files). Point to the snapshot-test pattern (§16.4, §21.1) as the required testing approach for a new adapter.
- Contribution guide: how to wire a new adapter into the CLI, run the test suite, and submit a PR (MIT, public repo).
- Non-goals: re-documenting each shipped adapter in full (those have their own packages/examples); backend ingestion internals.

## Acceptance criteria
- Page documents all six `AgentAdapter` methods plus `id`/`displayName`, matching the real interface signature.
- Page explains `normalizeEvent` mapping to `BirdyBeepEventType` and the canonical payload, with at least one concrete mapping example.
- Page states the non-destructive install rules (only managed entries, backup, idempotent, no repo-file tokens) and points to the snapshot-test requirement.
- A reader can follow the page to scaffold a new adapter and run its tests.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Verify the documented `AgentAdapter` signature against the real exported interface from `CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE` (compile a tiny stub adapter that implements exactly the methods/types the doc describes and assert it type-checks against the real interface — a mismatch fails). Run any code snippet in the page through the TypeScript compiler in a temp workspace and assert zero type errors. Confirm the referenced snapshot-test command/pattern actually exists and runs in the repo. Run the markdown link-checker over the page and assert all cross-links (to `CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE` types and example dirs) resolve.

## Dependencies & notes
- Depends on **EPIC: Public docs & examples** (epic root) and **AgentAdapter interface** (`CORE-ADAPTER-IFACE`) — the page documents that interface, so its final shape must exist first.
- Invariants to document for any new adapter: add only BirdyBeep-managed config entries, back up existing config, idempotent installs, tokens only via the shared store (never in repo files), hash/redact paths in `normalizeEvent`.

## References
- PRD §16.4 (adapter-development docs requirement), §16.1 (open-source / community-contribution rationale), §9.1 (`AgentAdapter` interface), §10.1–10.2 (event types + canonical payload), §9.5–9.7 (existing adapter mappings as examples), §21.1 (snapshot tests for config generation).

title: EPIC: Manual — HUMAN REQUIRED (publish & repo)
description:
> ⚠️ **HUMAN REQUIRED** — a human must perform this (real account / secret / billing / store / production action). Agents must NOT attempt it; prepare everything up to this gate and stop.

The gate epic for everything in `birdybeep-agent` that needs a real human identity, secret, or account action: creating the `@birdybeep` npm organization, holding publish credentials, and locking down the public GitHub repository. Agents build and verify the release tooling, CI, and repo-config-as-files up to the point of needing real credentials; a human then takes over to create accounts, paste secrets, and run the first publish.

## Scope
- Umbrella for the two human-gated chores in the public agent repo: first npm publish under `@birdybeep` and public GitHub repo hardening (branch protection, CODEOWNERS, templates, CI secrets).
- The human's job is orchestration + the irreversible account/secret actions only; agents must have everything else (release script, smoke test, CI matrix, template files, config docs) authored, tested, and ready to run before the gate.
- Non-goal: this epic does NOT cover writing the release/CI/adapter code itself (those are agent tickets `scripts/release.ts`, `scripts/smoke-test.ts`, and `CI matrix (macOS/Ubuntu/Windows)`); it only covers the human handoff that those feed into.
- Non-goal: no product-repo (`birdybeep`) secrets or store credentials live here — this is strictly the public MIT agent repo.

## Success criteria
- The `@birdybeep` npm org exists and `@birdybeep/cli` plus the `agent-core`, `claude-code`, `codex`, and `opencode` packages are published and installable by the public.
- The public `birdybeep-agent` GitHub repo is hardened: protected default branch, CODEOWNERS, issue/PR templates, and the CI secrets needed for the E2E matrix to run against staging.
- Every agent-prepared prerequisite (release script verified via dry-run, post-publish smoke test, cross-OS CI green) was confirmed working BEFORE the human performed any credentialed action.
- No token, npm automation token, or staging secret was ever committed to the repo.

## Testing (mandatory — agentic-first)
- Epic test bar: agents must prove each prerequisite green before the human acts — `scripts/release.ts` passes a real `--dry-run` (pack + version-bump simulation with nothing published), `scripts/smoke-test.ts` is wired to install-and-`doctor` against a published tarball in a clean temp HOME, and the OS-matrix CI (macOS/Ubuntu/Windows) is green on install/lint/typecheck/unit/snapshot.
- The human action is verified post-hoc by an agent: after first publish, run the smoke test against the real published package; after repo hardening, query the GitHub API to confirm protection rules and secrets exist (values never read).
- "It should publish" / "it should be locked down" is not acceptable — every claim is backed by a dry-run, a real install, or an API readback.

## Dependencies & notes
- Children: `[HUMAN] npm org @birdybeep + publish tokens + first publish` and `[HUMAN] Public repo settings + CI secrets + protection`.
- These children depend (cross-ticket) on the agent-authored `scripts/release.ts`, `scripts/smoke-test.ts`, and the `CI matrix (macOS/Ubuntu/Windows)` being done and verified first.
- Privacy/secret invariant: machine/installation tokens are hash-only server-side and shown once; nothing secret (npm tokens, CI secrets) is ever written into repo files. Installers and release tooling must be idempotent.
- This is the public MIT repo (`birdybeep-agent`); cross-repo links to the private `birdybeep` product repo are notes only, not hard dependencies.

## References
- PRD §16.3 (public repo `birdybeep-agent`, MIT, package layout `cli` / `agent-core` / `claude-code` / `codex` / `opencode`).
- PRD §16.4 (public agent repo requirements: install/uninstall/security docs, `doctor`, non-destructive config tests).

title: [HUMAN] npm org @birdybeep + publish tokens + first publish
description:
> ⚠️ **HUMAN REQUIRED** — a human must perform this (real account / secret / billing / store / production action). Agents must NOT attempt it; prepare everything up to this gate and stop.

Creates the public `@birdybeep` npm organization, issues the publish credentials, and performs the first real publish of `@birdybeep/cli` and its sibling packages so users can `npm install -g @birdybeep/cli`. This is the irreversible "claim the name + ship it" moment; an agent prepares and dry-run-verifies the entire release pipeline, and a human runs the final credentialed publish.

## What the human does vs. what an agent prepares
- **Human only:** sign in to npm with the real BirdyBeep account, create the `@birdybeep` scoped org, generate an npm automation/publish token (and, if used, add it to GitHub Actions secrets as the publish credential), then run the prepared release flow with `--no-dry-run` (or supply the token to CI) to publish for the first time. The human verifies 2FA and that the org/packages are public.
- **Agent prepares (and stops at the gate):** a finished, dry-run-clean `scripts/release.ts`; a `scripts/smoke-test.ts`; correct `package.json` `name`/`publishConfig.access: "public"`/`version`/`files`/`bin` for every package; an MIT `LICENSE`; READMEs; and a documented runbook of the exact commands the human will run. The agent must NEVER attempt the publish itself or handle the real token.

## Scope
- Publish the five public packages per PRD §16.3: `@birdybeep/cli`, `@birdybeep/agent-core`, `@birdybeep/claude-code`, `@birdybeep/codex`, `@birdybeep/opencode` — each marked `access: public` under the `@birdybeep` scope.
- Ensure `scripts/release.ts` does the version bump → build → publish, and is **dry-run by default** so an agent can validate the whole pipeline without publishing.
- Ensure `scripts/smoke-test.ts` installs the published CLI into a clean environment and runs `birdybeep doctor` to confirm the shipped artifact actually works.
- Non-goal: no Apple/Google store, RevenueCat, or product-backend credentials here; this ticket is solely the npm org + first publish of the public agent packages.

## Acceptance criteria
- The `@birdybeep` npm org exists and all five packages are published, public, and resolve via `npm view @birdybeep/cli` (and siblings).
- A clean-machine `npm install -g @birdybeep/cli` succeeds and the installed `birdybeep` binary runs (`birdybeep --version`, `birdybeep doctor`).
- The published versions match what `scripts/release.ts` produced; no package was published with private/internal-only access by mistake.
